coredata.dc:title: NIR II-triggered core-shell upconversion nanocomposites for peroxynitrite-boosted anti-infection against diabetic wound
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zekun
Fu, Xiaoyan
Dai, Chunxue
Yang, Bangjia
Wang, Weiyun
Fan, Cundong
Zhang, Pu
Sun, Jikui
Sun, Dongdong
coredata.dc:description: Infectious diseases due to bacterial resistance remains the great challenges in clinic, especially the antibiotic therapy of diabetic wound. Our previous studies verified that kanamycin-derived carbon-nanodots (KCDs) under light irradiation exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity by damaging bacterial biofilms in a reactive oxygen species (ROS)-dependent manner, and effectively promoted wound healing and tissue remodeling. In the present study, KCDs and nitric oxide donor (BNN6) were co-loaded in dendritic silica-coated NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 three-layer core–shell upconversion materials (USKB). Near-infrared light (NIR) triggered USKB to radiate ultraviolet light, and excited KCDs and BNN6 to produce superoxide anion and nitric oxide (NO), respectively. NO reacted with superoxide anion to form highly cytotoxic peroxynitrite (ONOO−) and boosted the antibacterial activity. The results showed that USKB under NIR-II exhibited enhanced antibacterial efficiency by peroxynitrite-boosted biofilm removel in vitro, and effectively promoted diabetic wound healing in vivo by relieving inflammatory environment and recruiting M2- macrophages. Importantly, USKB nanocomposites showed less side effects and good biocompatibility in vivo. Taken together, our findings present a superior paradigm for designing peroxynitrite-boosted anti-infection against diabetic wound.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of USKB. (A)Schematic diagram of the synthesis steps of USKB. (B) TEM image (Scale bar: 20 nm) and HRTEM magnified image (Scale bar: 2 nm) of KCDs. (C) TEM image (Scale bar: 100 nm) of UC. TEM images (Scale bar: 200 nm) of USK (D) and USKB (E). (F) HRTEM image of USKB (Scale bar: 100 nm). The green line is the boundary of NaYF4:Yb/Tm, the blue line is the boundary of NaYF4:Yb/Tm@NaYF4 and the red line is the boundary of USKB. (G) DF-STEM image (Scale bar: 100 nm) and element mapping for USKB. SEM images (Scale bar: 200 nm) of US (H) and USKB (I). (J) FTIR spectra of KCDs, BNN6, US, USK and USKB. (K) UCL spectra of UC, US, USK and USKB (from top to bottom). (L) XRD spectrum of USKB. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1 s (M), N 1 s (N) and Y 3d (O) orbitals of USKB. ESR spectra of NO (P) and O2 .- (Q) in USKB after laser irradiation for 0–10 min. (R) Fluorescence of L-tyrosine (ONOO– probe) after different treatments for ONOO– detection. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

USKB under NIR-II showed enhanced antibacterial activity in vitro. Time-dependent (A) or concentration-dependent (B) antimicrobial activity of Kana, US, USK and USKB after 10 min with or without laser treatment (980 nm, 1 W/cm2). The antimicrobial efficiency of Kana, US, USK and USKB with or without laser treatment was examined by the CFU method and expressed as colony count results (C) and typical representative images (D) of CFU (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (E) Live/dead fluorescent staining images of bacteria from Kana, US, USK and USKB groups with or without laser treatment (Scale bar: 10 μm). (F) SEM micrographs of bacteria from Kana, US, USK and USKB groups with or without laser processing (Scale bar: 1 μm). The red arrow points to nanomaterials on the surface of MRSA. The blue arrow points to the damaged cell membrane/wall of MRSA. PI (G) and DISC3-5 (H) fluorescence intensity of bacteria from Kana, US, USK and USKB groups with or without laser treatment (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (I) Schematic diagram of the antimicrobial mechanism based on USKB photodynamic triggering of triple ROS. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

USKB under NIR-II exhibited peroxynitrite-boosted anti-biofilm activity. (A) Fluorescence staining images of NO, O2 .- and ONOO– in each group of bacteria after 10 min of laser treatment or no treatment (Scale bar: 20 μm). (B) Concentration of triple reactive oxygen species in bacteria after laser treatment as a function of time. (C) Microscopic images of biofilm (Scale bar: 200 μm) crystalline violet staining, SEM microscopic images of biofilm (Scale bar: 5 μm) and quantitative analysis of biomass in biofilm (D) after different treatments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (E) Quantitative analysis of EPS in bacterial biofilms after different treatments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (F) Confocal 3D images of the anti-biofilm effect of laser treatment in the Kana, US, USK, USKB and USKB + SOD groups (SYTO 9 and PI co-staining). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Transcriptomics and RT-qPCR reveal the molecular mechanism of biofilm scavenging. Volcanic map analysis (A), GO pathway enrichment analysis (B), KEGG pathway enrichment analysis (C) and heat map analysis (D) of differential genes between bacteria treated by laser and USKB + laser. RT-qPCR analysis of genes related to biofilm synthesis (E) and energy metabolism (F). Effect of ATP content (H) and Glutathione balance (G) of MRSA after different treatments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (I) Schematic diagram of the molecular mechanism of triple ROS production by USKB (Laser) for biofilm scavenging and reduction of energy metabolism.

USKB under NIR-II promoted diabetic wound healing in vivo. (A) Schematic diagram of a diabetic mouse skin wound biofilm infection model and USKB photodynamic therapy. Photograph (B) and contraction diagram (C) of biofilm-infected wounds from day 0 to day 8 in the USKB group with or without laser treatment (n = 6). (D) Photographs of MRSA colonies from infected tissue on days 1 and 4 after treatment. (E) Changes in wound area and in vivo antimicrobial rate with treatment time in different treatment groups. (F) H&E staining, Masson staining and Tunel immunohistochemical staining of wound tissue at day 8 in different treatment groups (Scale bar: 100 μm). (G) Relative collagen density around the wound at day 8 in different treatment groups (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

USKB relieved inflammatory environment and recruited M2-macrophages in MRSA biofilm-infected diabetic wounds. (A) Immunofluorescence images of F4/80 (red), CD206 (green) and CD80 (purple) in infected wounds on day 8 after different treatments (Scale bar: 100 μm). Percentage of CD80 (B) and CD206 (C) area to F4/80 area after different treatments (n = 6, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (D) Immunohistochemical staining of IL-4, TNF-a and CD31 in wound tissue on day 8 in different treatment groups. Secretion levels of TNF-a (E) and IL-4 (F) in infected tissues of different treatment groups. The secretion levels of IL-6 (G), TGF-β (H) and VEGF (I) in the serum of mice in different treatment groups (n = 6, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (J) Quantitative analysis of microvessel density in different treatment groups (n = 6, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). The expression levels (K) and relative expression (L) of CD80, CD206, IL-4, IL-6, TNF-a, TGF-β, CD31 and Casepase-3 in the infected tissues were determined by western blotting after different treatments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (M) Schematic diagram of photodynamically induced USKB clearance of wound biofilm and promotion of macrophage polarization. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Excellent biocompatibility and biosafety of USKB. The results of live and dead fluorescence staining (A), mitochondrial membrane potential fluorescence staining (B) and flow cytometry (D) of fibroblasts (NIH/3T3 cells) after different treatments. Statistical results of live/dead cell ratio (C) and cell mitochondrial membrane potential flow cytometry (E) after different treatments. Effects of different treatments on blood biochemical indices ALT (F), AST (G), BUN (H), CRE (I), WBC (J) and PLT (K) in mice (n = 6, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).
Synthetic route and proposed mechanism of USKB against MRSA biofilm-infected diabetic wounds.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Infectious diseases due to bacterial resistance remains the great challenges in clinic, especially the antibiotic therapy of diabetic wound. Our previous studies verified that kanamycin-derived carbon-nanodots (KCDs) under light irradiation exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity by damaging bacterial biofilms in a reactive oxygen species (ROS)-dependent manner, and effectively promoted wound healing and tissue remodeling. In the present study, KCDs and nitric oxide donor (BNN6) were co-loaded in dendritic silica-coated NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 three-layer core–shell upconversion materials (USKB). Near-infrared light (NIR) triggered USKB to radiate ultraviolet light, and excited KCDs and BNN6 to produce superoxide anion and nitric oxide (NO), respectively. NO reacted with superoxide anion to form highly cytotoxic peroxynitrite (ONOO−) and boosted the antibacterial activity. The results showed that USKB under NIR-II exhibited enhanced antibacterial efficiency by peroxynitrite-boosted biofilm removel in vitro, and effectively promoted diabetic wound healing in vivo by relieving inflammatory environment and recruiting M2- macrophages. Importantly, USKB nanocomposites showed less side effects and good biocompatibility in vivo. Taken together, our findings present a superior paradigm for designing peroxynitrite-boosted anti-infection against diabetic wound.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) exhibits resistance to multiple classes of antibiotics and stability of biofilm formation, and is one of the common pathogens that cause serious consequences to human health and may even lead to death [1–3]. In biofilms, bacteria aggregate compactly by secreting extracellular polymers (EPS), which facilitate the development of bacterial antibiotic resistance and avoid immune system attack [4,5]. Biofilm infections stimulate the production of excessive inflammatory factors by the immune cells surrounding the wound, causing the wound to remain in an inflammatory phase for a long time and making healing difficult [6–9]. In particular, diabetic wounds are difficult to heal due to microangiopathy, persistent inflammatory response and dysfunctional epithelialization. Diabetic wounds combined with bacterial biofilm infections are extremely difficult to cure. An important factor is that excess glucose in the wound promotes bacterial proliferation and prevents the immune system from killing the bacteria, leading to long-term colonisation, which poses a serious threat to the quality of life and even to the lives of patients [10–12]. Currently, high-dose antibiotics remain the clinical treatment of choice for biofilm infections. However, these antibiotics often have limited efficacy and fewer antibiotic options are available, as the development of antibiotics has gone hand in hand with the development of drug-resistant bacteria [13,14]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for an effective strategy to treat diabetic wounds combined with biofilm infections.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has long been considered a promising non-drug-resistant treatment strategy for eliminating pathogenic bacteria because it does not target specific stages of metabolic pathways [15–17]. PDT usually generates singlet oxygen (1O2) by transferring energy to molecular oxygen after photosensitizer excitation, which oxidizes the biomolecules of pathogenic bacteria, eventually leading to the destruction of their structure and making it difficult to develop drug resistance. Most of the current photosensitizers show unsatisfactory anti-biofilm effects due to their poor water solubility resulting in low uptake by bacteria and difficulty in penetrating biofilms [18–22]. Carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted extensive research interest in the field of PDT because of their extremely small size and great permeability, extremely excellent optical properties, ease of surface chemical modification and unparalleled biocompatibility [23–25]. In a previous study we found that kanamycin sulfate-derived carbon dots (KCDs) exhibited excellent photodynamic and antibiotic activity. Their surface-modified compounds also showed significant inhibitory activity against S. aureus fatty acid synthase (FabH), thereby accelerating infected wound healing. Combined with some reported experimental results, it is known that 1O2 generated by electron-hole pairs on the surface of CDs under light induction is responsible for its antibacterial properties [26]. However, limited oxidation of antioxidants by 1O2 alone has been reported, which may lead to a lack of antibacterial and anti-biofilm activity [27]. Nitric oxide (NO) is not only a signaling molecule that plays a key role in many pathological processes, but is also considered an attractive antimicrobial agent because of its ability to both directly kill bacteria and promote healing of infected wounds [18,28]. It has been demonstrated in past studies that NO reacts rapidly with excess superoxide anion (O2
.- ) to form peroxynitrite anion (ONOO – ) [29,30]. ONOO – has a longer half-life and diffusion distance, extremely high oxidation and nucleophilic capacity compared to the 1O2 produced by conventional PDT, thus exhibiting greater oxidation and nitration capacity with extremely high antibacterial activity [31]. However, both KCDs and NO donors usually require direct excitation by ultraviolet (UV)/blue light at wavelengths of 300 nm-500 nm with limited tissue penetration depth, which limits their application in deep infected wounds or in vivo [32,33]. Lanthanide ion-doped upconversion luminescent materials (UCLM) can convert light from the near-infrared II region (NIR II) to UV/blue light and have been widely used [34–36]. However, UCLM without drug-loading properties are difficult to be used in biomedical applications. Zhang et al. prepared core–shell NaYF4: Yb3+/Er3+@SiO2@mSiO2 (Mesoporous silica) nanoparticles to explore the PDT efficiency of the loaded drugs via NIR light excitation [37]. Choi et al. developed egg yolk structure upconversion nanoparticles to produce 1O2 to inhibit drug-resistant bacteria through a combination of photodynamic and acoustic therapy [38]. Unfortunately, the limited upconversion efficiency and pore size (<3 nm) of UCML@mSiO2 mentioned above not only make the system low drug loading but also lead to poor PDT efficacy.

In this study, we developed dendritic silica-coated NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 three-layer core–shell upconversion luminescent material (US), which was also surface modified with amination in order to improve its loading rate of anionic drugs and adhesion to bacterial surfaces. US not only had large pores and excellent drug loading rate, but also could efficiently convert NIR II (∼980 nm) light to UV (∼365 nm) light for better penetration depth and avoiding potential damage from UV light. On this basis, KCDs-modified US (USK) and NO precursor BNN6 (N, N′-Di-sec-butyl-N, N′-dinitroso-1, 4-phenylene diamine)-filled USK were synthesized (USKB). We speculate that KCDs and BNN6 in USKB produce O2
.- and NO, respectively, and further form ONOO – after excitation by NIR II light. The in vitro antibacterial results showed that USKB exhibited significant antibacterial activity upon NIR II light activation. Mechanistic studies have shown that USKB produces three ROS under NIR II light excitation and exhibits efficient antimicrobial activity by disrupting the antioxidant system of MRSA, preventing bacterial ATP synthesis and disintegrating biofilms. Interestingly, the ONOO – seems to be pivotal in the antimicrobial activity. Importantly, USKB in vivo PDT accelerates diabetic wound healing in biofilm infections by rapidly removing biomass from the wound, recruiting more M2 macrophages, reducing the inflammatory response and promoting angiogenesis (Scheme 1 ). This work highlights a photodynamically induced triple ROS production and M2 macrophage recruitment strategy that effectively removes drug-resistant bacterial biofilms formed at infected wounds and accelerates wound healing.

All chemicals were used as received without purification. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99 %), Oleic acid (90 %), Ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 98 %), 1-octadecene (90 %), 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES, 98 %), Yttrium (III) chloride (YCl3, 99.99 %), Ytterbium (III) chloride (YbCl3, 99.9 %) and Thulium (III) chloride (TmCl3, 99.9 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Kanamycin sulfate (98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. N,N′-Di-sec-butyl-N,N′-dinitroso-1,4-phenylene diamine (BNN6, 95 %) was purchased from Ruixi Biological technology Co. Cetyl-trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99 %), Urea (98 %), n-Butanol (99.5 %) and Cyclohexane (99.5 %) were purchased from MackLin Inc. Fluorescent staining kits were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (China) Co. Mouse antibodies were purchased from Abcam (Shanghai) Trading Co. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA, ATCC 43300) was provided by the Department of Biopharmaceuticals, Anhui Agricultural University.

In a typical synthesis of NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ (30/1 mol%) monodisperse nanoparticles, 160 mg of YCl3, 84 mg of YbCl3 and 2.8 mg of TmCl3 were dissolved in the mixture of oleic acid (5 mL) and 1-octadecene (15 mL). The mixed solution was heated under nitrogen to 160 °C and held for 30 min until a clear yellow solution was obtained. After cooling to room temperature, 15 mL of a methanol solution containing 150 mg of NH4F and 100 mg of sodium hydroxide was added and stirred for 3 h. Subsequently, the solution was reheated to 300 °C and kept for 1 h to obtain NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ nanoparticles, and then 20 mL of ethanol was added to precipitate the nanoparticles and washed three times with ethanol. NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 core–shell was synthesized by growing a layer of NaYF4 on the surface of NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ by seed growth method. Similarly, 277 mg of YCl3 was dissolved in a mixture solution of oleic acid (5 mL) and 1-octadecene (15 mL) and heated to 160 °C under nitrogen protection and held for 30 min until a clear yellow solution was obtained. Then, the obtained NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ nanoparticles and 15 mL of methanol solution containing 150 mg of NH4F and 100 mg of sodium hydroxide were added to the above mixture. Subsequently, the solution was heated to 300 °C and reacted for 1 h to obtain NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 core–shell nanomaterials, and washed with ethanol three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dendritic mesoporous silica coated UCNPs (US)
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous phase was prepared by dissolving 400 mg of CTAB and 120 mg of urea in 6 mL of water. Meanwhile, the oil phase was prepared by dispersing 5 mg of UCNPs, 0.15 mL of n-butanol and 0.3 mL of TEOS in 6 mL of cyclohexane. After adding the aqueous phase to the oil phase and stirring vigorously at room temperature for 1 h, it was heated to 70 °C and reacted for more than 10 h to allow complete hydrolysis of TEOS. The surface amino-modified nanoparticles were obtained by continuing to add 30 μL of APTES to the reaction system for 2 h. The reaction product was collected by centrifugation (8500 rpm, 8 min) and washed three times with ethanol and pure water, respectively. Then, the US was redispersed in ethanolic solution containing 50 mg/mL of ammonium nitrate and refluxed at 60 °C for 12 h. The process was repeated three times to completely remove the residual CTAB from the US.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of US co-modified by KCDs and BNN6 (USKB)
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis of KCDs were performed according to the method we reported previously [26]. KCDs-modified US nanoparticles (USK) were synthesized by a similar method as described above. Briefly, 36 mg of KCDs, 400 mg of CTAB and 120 mg of urea were dissolved in 6 mL of water, mixed with the oil phase and stirred vigorously for 1 h at room temperature. The mixed solution was subsequently heated to 70 °C and reacted for more than 10 h to prepare USK. The reaction product was collected by centrifugation (8500 rpm, 8 min) and washed three times with ethanol and pure water, respectively. All supernatants were collected and the absorbance at 298 nm was measured using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer and the payload of KCDs in USK was calculated from the standard curve for KCDs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (DF-STEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope with an operated at 200 kV. The EDS and elemental mapping were performed on the equipped Oxford X-Max N150 detector. The surface morphology was observed by Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Thermo Nicoletteis50. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker EMX ESR spectrometer. ONOO – release was studied by using L-tyrosine, which could produce a characteristic fluorescence signal at 406 nm after being oxidized by ONOO –
[39]. UV–vis were obtained by a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The upconversion luminescence (UCL) emission spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the 980 nm laser. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and Barrett-Joyner-Halanda (BJH) method were used to calculate the specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples, respectively. The particle size distribution and zeta potential of the material were obtained by Brookhaven Zeta PALS. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded using a Bruker AXS D8 advanced diffractometer.

The conserved MRSA glycerol tubes were inoculated in LB medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to activate the strain, which was subsequently transferred to fresh Luria-Bertani (LB) medium and incubated for 6 h until the bacteria reached the logarithmic growth phase. In addition, LB medium containing MRSA (OD600 = 0.1) was added to 96-well plates and cultured at 37 ℃ for 2 days until biofilms formed at the bottom.

MRSA (1 × 106 CFU/mL) were incubated with different concentrations of Kana, UC, UCK and UCKB (2 μg/mL ∼ 128 μg/mL) for 2 h to bring the bacteria into full contact with the antimicrobial agents and then treated for 10 min under NIR laser irradiation (980 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions, using pbs-treated bacteria as controls. Then, the bacterial suspensions were diluted 100 times within 0 ∼ 240 min after laser treatment, respectively, and 100 μL of the suspensions were evenly coated on LB agar medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The total number of colonies was recorded for the projection of bacterial survival rate.

Fluorescence-based cell membrane permeability assay and live/dead staining assay further verified the antimicrobial efficiency of the antimicrobial agents. Briefly, MRSA (1 × 106 CFU/mL) was incubated with various antimicrobial agents (32 μg/mL) for 2 h, followed by treatment under NIR laser irradiation (980 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 10 min and incubation was continued for 60 min. Subsequently, it was co-stained with 5 μM SYTO9 (excitation wavelength: 485 nm/emission wavelength: 498 nm) and 30 μM PI (excitation wavelength: 535 nm/emission wavelength: 617 nm) for 15 min under dark conditions. The live/dead state of bacteria was recorded using a LEC-DMI8 laser confocal microscope, which exhibited green fluorescence and red fluorescence, respectively. Furthermore, MRSA was dark treated with DISC3-5 (5 μM) for 1 h followed by the addition of KCl (100 mM) to maintain ionic equilibrium. MRSA pre-loaded with fluorescent probes was treated with antimicrobial preparations in the same way as described above and the fluorescence intensity was detected using a SpectraMax M2 multifunctional enzyme marker.

Morphological changes of MRSA after different treatments were observed by SEM. Briefly, MRSA (1 × 106 CFU/mL) was incubated with 32 μg/mL of various antimicrobial agents for 2 h, followed by treatment under NIR laser irradiation (980 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 10 min. After continuing incubation for 60 min, the bacterial organisms were collected and then the bacterial forms were fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde overnight. Bacteria were then dehydrated in a gradient with different concentrations of ethanol (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, 100 %) and dried with a CO2 critical point drier. Finally, the bacteria were immobilized on conductive gel to observe their morphology by SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of intracellular ROS levels and types in bacteria
ce:section.ce:para:
The levels and types of intracellular ROS were identified by DHE (Dihydroethidium, O2
.- fluorescent probe), DAF-FM (4-Amino-5-methylamino-2′,7′-difluorofluorescein diacetate Diaminofluorescein-FM diacetate, NO fluorescent probe) and HPF (Hydroxyphenyl fluorescein, ONOO – fluorescent probe). Briefly, differently treated bacteria were collected at different time points after treatment and stained with 10 μM DAF-FM, DHE or HPF for 40 min at room temperature and protected from light, respectively. After staining, the bacteria were washed three times with PBS and the bacterial fluorescence intensity was measured by a multifunctional enzyme marker to assess the changes in the levels of different types of ROS in bacteria over time. In addition, bacteria that had undergone different treatments were collected at 30 min after treatment, stained as described above and then observed and recorded the fluorescence intensity of the bacteria by laser confocal microscopy.

Briefly, 200 μL of LB medium containing MRSA (OD600 = 0.1) was first placed in a 96-well plate and incubated in a 37 °C incubator for 2 days to form a stable biofilm at the bottom. Subsequently, the biofilm was co-incubated with Kana, US, USK or USKB for 2 h to make full contact with the biofilm, respectively. After irradiation with or without NIR light for 10 min, incubation with biofilm was continued for 60 min to fully disintegrate the biofilm, and then 0.5 % crystalline violet was added to stain the biofilm. Furthermore, the superoxide anion-specific scavenger SOD (0.2 U/mL) was added to assess the effect of ROS types on biofilm clearance. After staining for 15 min, the supernatant was discarded and washed three times by PBS, then 200 μL of anhydrous ethanol was added to dissolve the crystalline violet, and the absorbance of each treatment group was measured at 590 nm using a multi-functional enzyme marker.

B i o f i l m
r e m o v a l
r a t e
( % )
=
OD 590 (control group) - OD 590 (treatment group)
OD 590 (control group)
× 100 %.

Sterile coverslips were added to the bottom of 6-well plates and co-incubated with MRSA for 2 days to form a biofilm on the surface of the coverslips. Subsequently, the biofilms were treated as described above and the coverslips were rinsed with PBS to remove the disrupted biofilms. 5 μM SYTO9 and 30 μM PI dye mixture was used to stain the biofilms for 15 min, and the biofilm morphology of each treatment group was collected by laser confocal microscopy. In addition, the biofilms on the coverslips of each treatment group were fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde overnight and dehydrated with an ethanol gradient. Finally, the microstructure of the biofilm was recorded by SEM.

The EPS in MRSA biofilms was determined via phenol–sulfuric acid method. First, the groups of treated biofilms were well dispersed into PBS by sonication and the organisms were removed by centrifugation (5000 g, 5 min). Subsequently, the supernatant containing EPS was mixed equimolarly with the phenol solution, and a 95 % sulfuric acid solution was immediately added to the mixed solution and incubated for 30 min. Finally, the absorbance at 490 nm was measured by a multifunctional enzyme marker.

In vitro cytotoxicity of the antimicrobial agents was detected by live/dead cell fluorescence staining, cellular mitochondrial membrane potential method and flow cytometry. Briefly, NIH-3 T3 cells (1 × 106 cell/well) were inoculated into 6-well plates in an incubator containing 5 % CO2 for 24 h. After incubation, USK and USKB (32 μg/mL) were added, respectively, and incubated for another 24 h after 10 min of exposure with or without near-infrared light. Subsequently, the treated cells were stained according to the methods provided in the Cell Viability Kit (L3224) and the JC-1 Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Kit (M34152), and detected and recorded by inverted fluorescence microscopy or flow cytometry.

MRSA was co-incubated with USKB for 2 h, then irradiated by NIR light (980 nm, 1 W/cm2) for 10 min and incubation continued for 60 min. The group irradiated with NIR light alone for 10 min without USKB served as a control. Prior to extraction of total RNA, 2 mg/mL of lysozyme was added to the bacteria to lyse the cell wall. Total RNA was extracted through TRIzol instructions, and purified using Agencourt RNAClean XP Kit and RNase-Free DNase set. RNA sequence analysis was performed on Illumina HiSeq 2500 sequencer, and real-time data analysis was performed using data acquisition software provided by Illumina. The gene expression was calculated by edgeR package, genes with false discovery rate < 0.05 and |log2FC| ≥ 0.5 were considered as differential expressed genes (DEGs). To identify pathways associated with DEGs, we performed enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes using the GO and KEGG databases, respectively.

The bacterial treatment and RNA extraction process were the same as the RNA sequence analysis process. Furthermore, SOD (0.2 U/mL) was added simultaneously with USKB to the bacterial suspension to determine the effect of ROS on biofilm and ATP formation-related gene expression. RT-qPCR was performed on a StepOne real-time PCR system (ABI, USA) using Prime Script 1st Strand cDNA synthesis kit and SYBR Premix Ex Taq (TaKaRa, Japan). 16sRNA was selected as the internal reference gene. The primer sequences used in this study are shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treatment of MRSA-infected wounds in diabetic mice
ce:section.ce:para:
All animal procedures in this study were approved by the Second Affiliated Hospital of Shandong First Medical University for ethical review of experimental animals (2022–115). Male Kunming mice (6–8 weeks) were injected with streptozotocin (60 mg/kg) via intraperitoneal injection every 3 days for 3 weeks. Successful establishment of diabetic mice was suggested when blood glucose levels were greater than ∼ 16.7 mM. Diabetic mice were anesthetized with sodium pentobarbital (50 mg/kg), and a circular incision of approximately ∼ 1.0 cm in diameter was excised from the back and 20 μL of MRSA biofilm dispersion (108 CFU) was injected and incubated with the wound for 24 h to form a biofilm-infected diabetic wound. Subsequently, infected mice were randomly divided into 5 groups (n = 6): (1) model group, (2) laser treatment group, (3) USKB treatment group, (4) USK + Laser treatment group and (5) USKB + Laser treatment group. In the treatment group, 20 μL of PBS containing the antimicrobial agent (5 mg/kg) was applied uniformly on the wound (the model group was treated by 20 μL of PBS) and irradiated with or without NIR light for 10 min. On days 1, 2, 4, 6 and 8 after infection, the wounds of mice were recorded photographically and wound samples were collected, and the antimicrobial effect of the antimicrobial agents was assessed in vivo by colony counting.
A n t i b a c t e r i a l
r a t e
( % )
=
CFU (1) - CFU (n)
CFU (1)
× 100 %
where “n” represents the number of treatment days, such as 2, 4, 6 and 8.

Subsequently, blood samples were analyzed by Beckman LH750 automated hematology analyzer and ELASE assay kit (IL-6 (ab222503), IL-4 (ab100710), VEGF (ab209882), TGF-β (ab119557) and TNF-α (ab208348)) to detect blood biochemical parameters. Tissue samples were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde solution, followed by paraffin embedding and sectioning. Finally, histopathological analysis was performed by H&E staining. For immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence, sections were incubated with Anti-IL-4 (ab239508), Anti-CD31 (ab281583), Anti-TNF-a (ab307164), Anti-F4/80 (ab6640), Anti-CD206 (ab64693) and Anti-CD80 (ab254579) overnight and then treated with HRP goat anti-rabbit (ab6721), Alexa Fluor594-IgG (ab150160), Alexa Fluor488-IgG (ab150157) or TRITC (tetramethyl rhodamine)-IgG (ab7049) for 1 h. Finally, the nuclei were further stained with DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) and then recorded by Leica Aperio VERSA microscopy system.

On day 8 after treatment, proteins were isolated from tissues of the control and biofilm-infected groups and protein concentrations were determined using the BCA (Bicinchoninic Acid) kit (ab102536). Protein samples were separated by sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis and transferred to polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membranes. Afterwards, TBST (Tris Buffered Saline + Tween) containing 10 % skim milk was used for closure. The target protein bands on the membrane were visualized after incubation with primary antibody overnight and then with secondary antibody for 1 h, respectively. Grayscale analysis of the protein bands was quantified by Image J software.

All grouped data were presented as means ± standard deviation. Students’ t-test was used to compare the difference between two groups and one-way analysis of variance was applied to compare multigroup data using SPSS version 22. Probability (P) values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant, where *P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01 and *** P < 0.001.

In this study, we developed a dendritic silica-coated NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 triple core–shell upconversion luminescent material with KCDs modified on the silica scaffold and further amination modification on its surface to improve the loading rate to BNN6 and adhesion to bacterial surfaces (Fig. 1 A). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of KCDs in Fig. 1B shows that the KCDs are well-dispersed, spherical structures with a particle size distribution of 2–8 nm (Fig. S1A) and a lattice spacing of about 0.332 nm, comparable to that of graphite (002), which is consistent with our previously reported results [26]. The NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 core–shell structure is observed in Fig. 1C with a particle size of about 83.4 nm. As shown in Fig. 1D, the particle size of the US triple core–shell structure is about 240.7 nm and large pores can be observed, implying that the US may have a high drug loading capacity. USKB was prepared by co-modifying US with KCDs and BNN6, and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results can be observed that USKB pores were filled (Fig. 1E) with a particle size of 299.2 nm, the increased size of which was associated with the amination modification of the US surface and the loading of KCDs and BNN6. HRTEM (Fig. 1F), dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (DF-STEM) and elemental mapping (Fig. 1G) results further validate the three-layer core–shell structure, with Yb and Tm elements attributed to the NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+ core structure, S elements attributed to KCDS, and N elements mainly attributed to the BNN6 modification. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) results of US (Fig. 1H) and USKB (Fig. 1I) further confirmed that the pores of US were significantly reduced after modification and loading by KCDs and BNN6, respectively. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments quantify the changes in pore size, as shown in Fig. S1B, where the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms all exhibited a Type IV pattern. The nitrogen adsorption curve of US rises more significantly above a relative pressure of 0.4, indicating mesoporous capillary coalescence. In contrast, the nitrogen adsorption curve for USKB rises less, indicating a reduced mesoporous structure. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore capacity of US were 500.25 m2/g and 0.72 cm3/g, respectively, with a pore diameter of approximately 9.69 nm (Fig. S1C). In contrast, the surface area and pore capacity of USKB decreased to 197.34 m2/g and 0.26 cm3/g, respectively, with a pore size of approximately 3.44 nm. The BET surface area and pores of USKB were significantly reduced, indicating that the pores of US were filled with KCD and BNN6, and these results are consistent with the observations of TEM and SEM. The drug loading rates for KCDs (Fig. S1D) and BNN6 (Fig. S1E) in USKB were calculated from the standard curves and regression equations for KCDs and BNN6 to be 28.3 % and 21.5 %, respectively. UV–visible spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) were used to reveal the success of each chemical modification step. As shown in Fig. S1F, the broad peak of USKB at 307 nm is attributed to the blue shift of the absorption peak of US at 318 nm and the red shift of the absorption peaks of BNN6 at 270 nm and KCDs at 298 nm. The N-H bending vibration peak at 1628.28 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of USKB was attributed to the N-H bending vibration peak at 1597.03 cm−1 for KCDs and 1636.84 cm−1 for US, while the peak at 1511.99 cm−1 was attributed to the blue shift of the benzene ring backbone vibration peak of BNN6 at 1509.87 cm−1 (Fig. 1J). Furthermore, all successful chemical modifications lead to changes in the zeta potential of the nanomaterials (Fig. S1G). Therefore, we further corroborated the successful synthesis of USKB by UV–visible spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy and zeta potential. Subsequently, we explored the upconversion luminescence spectra (UCL) of NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+, NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4, US and USKB. As shown in Fig. 1K, the upconversion materials exhibited the strongest emission spectra at 365 nm at 980 nm excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the photoconversion efficiency intensity was enhanced by 16.5 % after NaYF4 coating NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+, which contributed to the effective excitation of KCDs and BNN6. However, the photoconversion efficiency intensity of USKB decreased by 23.4 % compared to NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4, which was associated with dendritic silica modification and drug loading.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of USKB is shown in Fig. 1L. All the diffraction peaks attributed to the hexagonal phase of NaYF4 (JCPDS No. 016–0334) can be found, while the broad peaks at 2θ = 22° are mainly from amorphous silica and carbon nanodots. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis in Fig. S2A shows that USKB consists of C 1 s (Fig. 1M), O 1 s, N 1 s (Fig. 1N), Si 2p (Fig. S2B), S 2p, Y 3d (Fig. 1O), Yb 4d (Fig. S2C) and Tm 4d (Fig. S2D) peaks. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s with peaks at 284.2, 284.7, 285.1 and 286.2 eV can be attributed to C-C/C = C, C-N, C-O and C = O, respectively [40]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s with three peaks at 398.8, 400.6 and 402.3 eV are attributed to –NO, graphite N and –NH-, where graphite N and –NO are derived from KCDs and BNN6, respectively [26]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Y 3d with peaks at 153.3 and 154.2 eV are attributed to Y3+. To verify that BNN6 and KCDs in USKB produce ROS under NIR light excitation, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was used to detect NO (Fig. 1P) and O2
.- (Fig. 1Q) captured by 2-Phenyl-4, 4,5,5-tetramethylimidazoline-1-oxyl 3-oxide (PTIO) and 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). Further, the ability to detect ONOO – production from NO and O2
.- using L-tyrosine as an ONOO – fluorescent probe (Fig. 1R). The yields of NO, O2
.- and ONOO – increased with increasing irradiation time, and the yields of triple ROS were significantly higher after 10 min of laser irradiation than those at 3 min of laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. S3A and S3B, USKB changed from a light brown solution/powder to a light red colour upon laser irradiation due to the release of NO from the BNN6 structure resulting in its conversion to the red compound (N,N'-Bis(1-methylpropyl)-1,4-phenylenediamine). In addition, analysis of the thermodynamic stability of USKB revealed that the dimensions of USKB with or without laser irradiation did not change significantly within 72 h (Fig. S4A), indicating that the dendritic silica-coated upconversion drug delivery system has high stability. Further analysis revealed that USKB can continuously release KCDs within 72 h and the release rate is higher at pH 6.0 and after laser irradiation, which implies that USKB may sustain antimicrobial activity through long-term slow release of KCDs in the wound microacidic environment (Fig. S4B). In summary, we successfully constructed the US upconversion luminescent materials co-modified with BNN6 and KCDs, which can emit strong UV light at 365 nm under the excitation of near-infrared light at 980 nm and further induce the production of O2
.- and NO by KCDs and BNN6, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic enhanced antibacterial activity of USKB in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
In a previous report, we found that KCDs exhibited significant antibacterial effects in vitro by retaining antibiotic activity and generating photodynamic properties of carbon points [26]. Inspired by the excellent antibacterial activity of KCDs and the photodynamic excitation of USKB to generate NO and O2
.- radicals, the antibacterial performance of USKB against MRSA was evaluated in vitro. The antimicrobial activity of different concentrations of antimicrobial agents with or without NIR irradiation for 0–12 min is shown in Fig. 2 A and B. USKB exhibited the strongest antibacterial activity against MRSA compared to Kana, US and USK after 10 min of NIR irradiation, and the antibacterial activity increased with concentration dependence, exceeding 99 % at 32 μg/mL. Although a 980 nm laser can excite USKB more effectively, the potential laser-induced heating effect still cannot be ignored [28]. As shown in Fig. S5A, the temperature of the pure water reached 41.5 °C after 12 min of laser irradiation (which is similar to the temperature of the USKB solution at 42.3 °C after 10 min of laser irradiation) and its antibacterial efficiency was 6.5 % (Fig. S5B), implying that the high antibacterial efficiency of USKB may be partly due to the photothermal effect. However, we do not consider this effect to be significant. This is because within 6 min of laser irradiation, when the temperature of the USKB solution was still low, the antibacterial activity reached 75.2 %, which could be attributed to the triple ROS generated by USKB after laser irradiation. The colony-forming unit (CFU) results also showed that no significant antibacterial activity was detected in the control group with or without NIR irradiation, suggesting that NIR light alone (980 nm) has no significant effect on bacterial growth (Fig. 2C and D). However, UV light alone (365 nm) had a significant effect on bacterial growth (Fig. S5B). Furthermore, all antimicrobial agents that were not irradiated with NIR showed limited antimicrobial activity, which is related to the multidrug resistance properties of MRSA. Conversely, both USK and USKB showed significant antibacterial activity after NIR irradiation, with the total number of colonies decreasing from ∼ 1 × 106 to ∼ 2.8 × 105 and ∼ 7.5 × 103 CFU/mL, respectively. In addition, long-lasting bacterial inhibitory activity against MRSA (<104 CFU/mL) remained after USKB photodynamic treatment, which was associated with the slow release of KCDs (Fig. S5C). The photodynamic antibacterial effect of USKB was further evaluated using live/dead bacterial fluorescence staining. In Fig. 2E, it was observed that all MRSA after dark treatment showed green fluorescence emission. However, the USKB group after NIR irradiation showed almost all red fluorescence emission (Fig. 2G), indicating the eradication effect on MRSA. These results are consistent with the results of MIC and CFU experiments, indicating that USKB has photodynamically enhanced antibacterial activity.

SEM was used to observe the adsorption characteristics of USKB on MRSA and the effect on membrane integrity. As shown in Fig. S6, the amounts of US attached to the bacterial surface without the amination modification was significant less than that of US, USK and USKB after the amination modification. This may be due to the positive charge of the amino-modified US, which interacts electrostatically with the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane. As shown in Fig. 2F, the dark-treated groups of bacteria exhibited smooth and intact morphology. Bacteria in the US, USK and USKB groups showed different degrees of surface damage after NIR excitation, especially USKB resulted in severe rupture of the bacterial membrane/cell wall. The observation was confirmed by the DISC3-5 (3,30-dipropylthiadicarbo cyanine iodide) fluorescent probe assay, which showed almost no fluorescence in bacteria with intact cell membranes. However, when the cell membrane was damaged, DISC3-5 was released and thus exhibited green fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the degree of cell membrane damage. Similarly, significantly increased levels of MRSA green fluorescence were detected in the USK and USKB groups after NIR irradiation, indicating that the cell membrane was severely damaged (Fig. 2H). Interestingly, with or without laser irradiation, the antimicrobial agent can be tightly adsorbed to the bacterium due to its positively charged amino modification on the surface, which contributes to better antimicrobial activity. These results confirmed that USKB photodynamic disruption of the cell membrane and cell wall of MRSA exhibited significant antimicrobial effects. This can be attributed to the upconversion material at the core of USKB converting NIR to 365 nm UV light, which excites KCDs and BNN6 to produce O2
.- and NO, respectively, which further generates ONOO – (Fig. 2I). Triple ROS were sensitive to multidrug-resistant MRSA by causing widespread oxidative damage in bacteria rather than targeting specific stages of the metabolic pathway.

The excellent in vitro antibacterial activity indicated that a photodynamic antibacterial platform based on laser upconversion has been successfully established. The identification of triple ROS by staining verified whether they play an important role in the antimicrobial efficiency of USKB, thus further confirming the antimicrobial mechanism of triple ROS against drug-resistant MRSA. As shown in Fig. 3 A, almost none of the three ROS were detected as fluorescence signals in all treatment groups without laser irradiation, while all three ROS were significantly expressed in the USKB group after laser treatment, which showed a significant increase in fluorescence intensity. We found that the three ROS were produced in a certain time sequence (Fig. 3B). Firstly, bacteria in the USKB treatment group reached peak NO and O2
.- concentrations after 3 and 5 min of laser irradiation, respectively. Subsequently, the ONOO – concentration in the bacterium increased significantly, while the NO and O2
.- concentrations decreased. In particular, the addition of SOD (superoxide dismutase, a specific scavenger of O2
.- to USKB (Laser) significantly inhibited the production of O2
.- and ONOO – , resulting in a decrease in its antimicrobial activity from > 99 % to ∼ 45.4 % (Fig. S7A). However, USK produced only O2
.- under laser with an antibacterial activity of 66.2 %, thus ONOO – was critical in the photodynamic antibacterial activity of USKB. In Fig. S7B-C, the addition of SOD in the USKB (Laser) treated group reduced the leakage of bacterial protein and DNA from 0.365 mg/mL to 0.211 mg/mL and 285.7 ng/μL to 166.4 ng/μL, respectively, further demonstrating the synergistic antibacterial advantage of triple reactive oxygen species.

The large number of extracellular polymers, complex biofilm microenvironment and different metabolic states of the bacterium constitute the biofilm of MRSA, which has serious negative effects on the treatment with antibiotics or other antimicrobial agents, so it is important to develop efficient and rapid biofilm removal agents [41,42]. Here we propose the possibility of triple ROS concentration dependent removal of biofilms. The efficacy of photodynamic biofilm removal by USKB was verified by crystalline violet staining experiments and SEM observation of biofilm. As shown in Fig. 3C, USK and USKB resulted in MRSA biofilms exhibiting significant structural damage after NIR irradiation, with biofilm clearance of 61.4 % and 95.9 %, respectively (Fig. 3D). Their EPS were reduced by 2.6- and 7.5-fold, respectively, compared with the control group. In contrast, SOD reduced USKB (Laser) biofilm clearance to 27.8 %, while EPS increased 2.9-fold compared to USKB (Laser) alone (Fig. 3E). The three-dimensional (3D) laser confocal images of the biofilm further showed (Fig. 3F) that the control biofilm was dense and almost all live cells (SYTO 9, green fluorescent) after laser treatment, while the USKB treated biofilm was significantly reduced and almost all dead cells (PI, red fluorescent). These results indicate that NIR can stimulate USKB to produce triple ROS and exhibit significant biofilm scavenging activity. Further, the efficient biofilm scavenging activity of USKB (Laser) is closely related to the synergistic effect of triple ROS, whereas the use of either O2
.- or NO alone leads to a significant decrease in anti-biofilm activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular mechanisms of USKB photodynamic antibacterial and antibiofilm
ce:section.ce:para:
The underlying molecular mechanism of USKB photodynamically activated antibiofilm and antibacterial activity was investigated by RNA sequencing. Compared with the laser group alone, 375 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) expression was down-regulated and 907 DEGs expression was up-regulated in the USKB (Laser) group, respectively (Fig. 4 A). In addition, we performed enrichment analysis of the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) and Gene Ontology (GO) pathways separately to identify the top 12 affected pathways. Pathways such as oxidoreductase activity and cell wall macromolecular metabolites were enriched in the GO analysis, suggesting that USKB (Laser) may cause oxidative damage and cell wall disruption to bacteria (Fig. 4B). Pathways such as biofilm formation, phosphotransferase system (PTS), tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and fatty acid biosynthesis were enriched in KEGG analysis, suggesting that USKB (Laser) may have a significant effect on bacterial biofilm formation and metabolism (Fig. 4C). Transcriptome heat map analysis revealed that USKB (Laser) significantly upregulated genes related to the promotion of MRSA biofilm breakdown and downregulation of energy metabolism and fatty acid synthesis, which severely affected bacterial resistance development and growth metabolism (Fig. 4D). The relative expressions of agrA, ask (Fig. 4E), agrC (Fig. S8A), lacB, lacD (Fig. 4F) and fabH (Fig. S8B) genes were further verified by real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR). Importantly, biofilm catabolism and bacterial energy metabolism-related gene expression were significantly downregulated and upregulated, respectively, when SOD scavenged O2
.- and reduced ONOO – , thus triple ROS are critical in terms of bacterial biofilm scavenging and affecting energy metabolism. The bacterial adenosine triphosphate (ATP) content was detected as shown in Fig. 4G. The bacterial ATP content decreased from ∼ 1.37 nM to ∼ 0.42 nM after USKB (Laser) treatment, compared to an increase in ATP content to ∼ 1.04 nM after USKB (Laser) + SOD treatment. In addition, an important marker of bacterial oxygen damage is the disruption of glutathione homeostasis [43]. As shown in Fig. 4H, the ratio between oxidized glutathione (GSSG) and reduced glutathione (GSH) was significantly increased in USKB (Laser)-treated bacteria, implying a breakdown of the ROS defence system, which may be responsible for bacterial cell membrane rupture, DNA leakage, protein leakage and reduced ATP synthesis. Interestingly, in a previous study we demonstrated by molecular docking experiments that KCDs surface chemical modification has significant inhibitory activity against the fatty acid synthase FabH of S. aureus. In this study, it was further found that USKB (Laser) could reduce the expression of fabH through triple ROS, thus achieving significant antibacterial activity. These results indicate that USKB photodynamic production of triple ROS can further lead to the destruction of bacterial biofilms, reduced ATP synthesis and glutathione imbalance by up-regulating the clearance of biofilm-related genes and down-regulating the genes related to energy metabolism and fatty acid synthesis, thus showing high antibacterial activity (Fig. 4I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: USKB photodynamic treatment of biofilm infection in diabetic wounds
ce:section.ce:para: One of the main reasons for the difficulty of diabetic wound healing was superimposed infection and delayed repair due to biofilm [44]. Here, a diabetic mouse model was established by streptozotocin and a wound model of MRSA biofilm infection was further constructed (Fig. 5 A). Specifically, mice wounds were incubated with MRSA biofilm for 1 day and then sampled to determine infection. After successful infection, mice were treated differently by group. Wound photographs (Fig. 5B) and area (Fig. 5C) were recorded every two days and the total number of colonies on each wound surface was quantified by the CFU method on days 1 and 4 after infection (Fig. 5D). On the first day after infection, high levels of bacteria were detected in all mouse wounds, indicating that the biofilm infection model was successfully constructed. The diabetic mice in the model group maintained high levels of total wound bacteria and had difficulty in wound healing, with wound healing and antimicrobial rates of ∼ 31.4 % and ∼ 31.2 %, respectively, after 8 days of infection. In contrast, the wound bacterial levels decreased significantly and the healing rate accelerated after USKB photodynamic treatment, with wound healing and antimicrobial rates of ∼ 90.7 % and ∼ 99.9 % (Fig. 5E), respectively, after 8 days of infection. Interestingly, as shown in Figs. S9A-S9D, the wound antimicrobial efficiency and healing rate (the wound area was reduced by 71.7 % on day 8) were significantly lower in the USKB + Laser (365 nm) treatment group than that of the USKB + Laser (980 nm) treatment group. This is due to the greater tissue penetration of the 980 nm excitation wavelength compared to the shorter 365 nm wavelength, which may be more conducive to the treatment of infected wounds in the deep, thus exhibiting stronger in vivo antimicrobial and wound healing promoting effects. In addition, wound tissues from different treatment groups were histologically stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), Masson and Tunel on the 8th day after infection. As shown in Fig. 5F, the skin structure in the infected group or the laser-treated group alone was severely abnormal, as seen by massive inflammatory cell infiltration, apoptosis of skin tissue cells and loss of collagen fibers. However, the skin structure was significantly improved in the USK and USKB photodynamic treatment groups compared to the model group, where intact epidermis, dermis and sebaceous glands could be observed, inflammatory cell infiltration and apoptosis of skin tissue were significantly reduced, collagen fibers at the wound tissue were significantly increased, and collagen density was increased by ∼ 3.9-fold and ∼ 7.3-fold, respectively (Fig. 5G). These results suggest that the USKB photodynamic therapy group most significantly accelerates wound healing by rapidly removing the bacterial count of wound biofilm infections, reducing inflammatory factor infiltration and increasing collagen fiber deposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: USKB photodynamic activation of macrophage polarization
ce:section.ce:para: Persistent inflammation caused by repeated bacterial irritation is an important reason for the difficulty of diabetic wound healing after MRSA biofilm infection. The difficulty is that MRSA biofilms are severely resistant to a wide range of clinically used antibiotics, and the development of resistance continues [44,45]. Macrophage polarization plays a key regulatory role in different stages of tissue repair. M1 macrophages are associated with the development of inflammation in infected tissues, whereas M2 macrophages are associated with the secretion of anti-inflammatory factors and the promotion of wound repair [46,47]. Therefore, we further evaluated the effect of USKB photodynamic therapy on macrophage polarization in tissues. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the infected group of mice had the highest percentage of M1 macrophages (CD80 and F4/80) in their tissues, indicating that macrophages were mainly differentiated into M1 macrophages (Fig. 6B). Conversely, the highest percentage of M2 macrophages (CD206 and F4/80) were infected in the tissues of mice in the USKB (Laser)-treated group (Fig. 6C). In addition, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α, Fig. 6D and 6E) and interleukin-6 (IL-6, Fig. 6G) levels were significantly decreased in wound tissue or serum, while interleukin-4 (IL-4, Fig. 6D and F) and transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β, Fig. 6H) levels were significantly increased in the USKB (Laser) group of mice compared with other treatment groups. As shown in Fig. 6K and L, Western blotting (WB) results verified high expression of CD206, TGF-β and IL-4 and low expression of CD80, TNF-a and IL-6 in infected tissues after USKB treatment, which further confirmed that USKB photodynamically activated the M2 phenotype of macrophages, which would reduce the inflammatory response and promote wound healing. In general, the reduction of M1 macrophages and their secretion of pro-inflammatory factors shortens the wound healing process. Neovascularization is essential for nutrient and oxygen transport to maintain fibroblast proliferation, collagen synthesis, and re-epithelialization. Therefore, we examined platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (CD31) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) in wound tissue or serum. Immunohistochemistry, serum assays and WB results all showed that CD31 (Fig. 6I) and VEGF (Fig. 6J) levels were lower in the infected mice than in the USKB (Laser)-treated group, suggesting that USKB photodynamic has the potential to promote wound angiogenesis and accelerate wound healing. These results suggest that MRSA biofilm infection causes macrophages to differentiate primarily into M1 macrophages and secrete pro-inflammatory factors, resulting in a prolonged inflammatory environment that makes wound healing difficult (Fig. 6M). In contrast, USKB photodynamic therapy resulted in a polarization of macrophages to an anti-inflammatory phenotype and promoted angiogenesis to accelerate wound healing.

MRSA, a representative pathogenic drug-resistant bacterium, can resist a wide range of common antibiotics [48]. Rapid developments are being made to develop alternative antibiotic nanoparticles, with the advantage of not causing bacterial resistance. Li et al. [49] report the de novo design of the d-electron of the active centre by the electron transfer in vanadium-based AEs (VOx-AE) via Zn–O–V bridge for efficient ROS-catalysis. When catalyzing H2O2, the VOx-AE shows enhanced catalytic eradication of drug-resistant bacteria. However, such enzyme-mimetic catalysts have insufficient bactericidal activity at very low concentrations, while high concentrations of antimicrobial materials tend to increase the risk of toxicity [50]. When the VOx-AE concentration is 0.2 mg/mL with H2O2 (100 μM) was used, the VOx-AE + H2O2 displays the highest antibacterial rate up to 95 ± 5 %, In contrast, at USKB concentrations up to 32 μg/mL, the antibacterial activity reached 99 % after 10 min of 980 nm laser irradiation. Thus, USKB has a more efficient antibacterial activity. Sun et al. [28] combined upconversion nanoparticles and porphyrin MOFs before being incorporated into poly (vinylidene fluoride) with L-arginine to obtain nanocomposite membrane (UCNP@PCN@LA-PVDF). Under near-infrared light irradiation (980 nm), this UCNP@PCN@LA-PVDF induces NO production from loaded arginine and generates sufficient ROS to ultimately achieve NO-assisted PDT antimicrobial behaviour. However, the authors overlooked the intrinsic mechanism by which NO enhances the antimicrobial activity of ROS and the intrinsic reason for promoting wound healing. It is important to emphasize that our study shows that the reaction of NO and O2
.- to generate ONOO – is important for enhancing antimicrobial activity. Secondly, USKB photodynamic generation of triple ROS has concentration-dependent biofilm scavenging activity. Finally, USKB photodynamic therapy leads to polarisation of macrophages to an anti-inflammatory phenotype and promotes angiogenesis to accelerate wound healing. Zhang et al [51]. developed the upconversion nanoparticle as the core, coated with mesoporous silica, for loading photosensitizers, NO donors and bacterial targeting molecules. This nanoplatform (UCNA Ns) produced NO, O2
.- and ONOO – upon laser irradiation at 808 nm and had more significant antibacterial activity in vitro and in vivo. However, the dendritic silica of USKB has greater pore capacity, pore size and drug loading capacity compared to UCNA Ns, resulting in higher antimicrobial activity. Importantly, USKB is excited by near infrared light at 980 nm and its ability to penetrate deeper into tissues may be more advantageous for the treatment of deep infections.

For safety reasons, the USKB-treated group was evaluated for potential toxicity in vitro and in vivo before and after laser irradiation. Live/dead cell staining, JC-1 mitochondrial membrane potential staining and flow cytometry were used to assess its toxicity to mouse fibroblasts (NIH-3 T3), respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 A, there was no significant cell death after both USK (Laser) and USKB (Laser) treatment, and the cell survival rate was above ∼ 90 % (Fig. 7C). The decrease in mitochondrial membrane potential is a hallmark of early apoptosis. In Fig. 7B, almost all cells in treatment groups were red fluorescent (JC-1 aggregates), and no significant green fluorescence (JC-1 monomers) was observed, indicating that the mitochondrial membrane potential of the cells did not decrease significantly at this time. Further quantification by flow cytometry showed that the mitochondrial membrane potential decreased by less than ∼ 10 % after USK (Laser) and USKB (Laser) treatment (Fig. 7D and E). These results indicated that USKB photodynamic treatment was not significantly cytotoxic to NIH-3 T3. After execution of the mice on day 8 of the wound healing experiment, serum and major organs were collected and assessed for blood biochemical parameters and tissue damage. As shown in Fig. 7F–K, the differences in liver function indicators, including alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and aspartate transaminase (AST), kidney function indicators, including blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CRE), white blood cell (WBC) and platelet (PLT) between each treatment group and the control group were not statistically significant. H&E staining showed no significant differences in the histological structure of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) between each treatment group and the control group, and no inflammatory damage or organic changes were observed (Fig. S10). Similarly, Xiong et al. [52] systematically studied the long-term toxicity of PAA-coated NaYF4:Yb,Er nanoparticles (15 mg/kg) administered intravenously injected into mice by observing the behaviour, body weight, histology and hematology, and serum biochemistry of the animals. The results showed that no abnormal indicators were found in all mice after 115 days of injection. In this work, USKB (5 mg/kg) was applied to diabetic wounds, and not only was the effective dose much lower than the safe dose reported, but skin wound administration may be safer compared to intravenous administration. In summary, both in vitro and in vivo experiments confirm that USKB (Laser) exhibits very low cytotoxicity and high biosafety.

In this study, we developed dendritic silica-coated NaYF4: Yb3+/Tm3+@NaYF4 three-layer core–shell upconversion luminescent material (US), which was also surface modified with amination in order to improve its loading rate of anionic drugs and adhesion to bacterial surfaces. On this basis, KCDs and BNN6 were co-modified in porous structures of US to develop upconversion luminescent nanosystems for photodynamic treatment of bacterial biofilm infections. The introduction of USKB can convert NIR light into UV light, which can effectively excite both KCDs and BNN6 to produce O2
.- and NO, respectively, and further oxidize them to the highly toxic ONOO – . USKB photodynamic therapy exhibits significant in vitro antibacterial activity by damaging bacterial cell membranes and cell walls, removing bacterial biofilms, and reducing bacterial energy and fatty acid synthesis. Transcriptome sequencing and RT-qPCR further validated that USKB photodynamic generation of triple ROS significantly downregulated the expression of genes related to biofilm formation (agrA, agrC, ask), energy metabolism (lacB, atpA) and fatty acid synthesis (fabH). Importantly, the formation and synergistic action of triple ROS exhibits a critical role in antibacterial and biofilm removal, as the bioactivity is significantly reduced by NO or O2
.- alone. Furthermore, the number of bacteria and apoptotic cells in the wound tissue were significantly reduced after USKB photodynamic treatment in a diabetic mouse wound biofilm infection model. More importantly, USKB (Laser) can shorten the M1 macrophage polarization cycle and promote earlier polarization of macrophages to an anti-inflammatory phenotype (M2 macrophages), whose reparative secretions significantly improve the wound inflammatory microenvironment through a paracrine mechanism, promoting angiogenesis and collagen deposition, which subsequently promotes diabetic wound healing. Overall, USKB photodynamic therapy offers a promising strategy for efficient removal of drug-resistant bacterial biofilm infections and promotion of diabetic wound healing.

The data supporting the findings from this study are available within the manuscript and the supplementary information. Any remaining raw data are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
